title: Deep dive: Artifact concept — types, lifecycle, RAG retrieval, relationship to PM artifacts
description:
The current ArtifactStore trait (gyres-core/src/artifact.rs) defines artifacts as implicit side-effects of agent work (decisions, documentation, code changes). But 'artifact' is overloaded — PM artifacts (PRDs, ADRs, SPECs, ROADMAPs) are also artifacts. FDD/BDD produce artifacts. The agent's own work products are artifacts.

Questions to resolve:
- What is the taxonomy of artifacts? Code artifacts vs document artifacts vs decision artifacts vs PM artifacts?
- How do Artifact types relate to the PM hierarchy (gyres-wy3)? Is a Task itself an artifact? Is an ADR?
- Should Artifact have a type system (enum of known kinds) or stay stringly-typed (kind: String)?
- What's the lifecycle? Created → stored → searchable → consolidated → archived?
- How does RAG retrieval work in practice? What makes an artifact useful as future context?
- Relationship to MemoryStore: when does an artifact become a memory? Are they the same thing with different access patterns?
- The 'implicit side-effects' concept (docs/src/concepts/implicit-side-effects.md) — how does ArtifactStore make this concrete?
- Should artifacts be versioned? (Agent updates a decision — is that a new artifact or an update?)

Refs: docs/src/concepts/implicit-side-effects.md, gyres-core/src/artifact.rs, gyres-wy3 (PM workflow)

title: InMemoryBackend: zero-dep default satisfying all Store traits
description: Implement InMemoryBackend as the default backend. Must satisfy StateStore + MemoryStore + TaskStore + ArtifactStore. Uses HashMap/BTreeMap internally. No external dependencies. Thread-safe (RwLock-wrapped). Used for testing and as the GyreContext::minimal() default. Semantic search: no vector search (return empty results or exact match only). Graph traversal: basic adjacency list. This is the reference implementation that proves the Store traits work.

title: Backend trait design: interface, health check, connection management, schema migration
description: Design the Backend marker/base trait. What operations does every backend support? Health check (is the DB reachable?). Connection pooling (shared pool across stores). Schema migration (how do stores evolve their tables/collections?). Backend lifecycle: init, health_check, shutdown. How does one Backend instance satisfy multiple Store traits simultaneously?
close_reason: Backend trait implemented in gyres-core/src/backend.rs. Interface: name(), health_check(), shutdown(). StorageConfig enum (Memory/Sqlite/Surreal) with serde support. Store-abstraction doc updated to reflect single gyres-store crate (ADR 0016).

title: Storage factory: StorageConfig → Stores bundle, feature-gating strategy
description: Design the factory that creates all stores from config. StorageConfig enum: InMemory, JsonFiles, Sqlite, Surreal. Feature flags: backend-sqlite, backend-surreal, backend-memory, backend-json, backend-all. The factory returns a Stores struct with Arc<dyn StateStore>, Arc<dyn MemoryStore>, etc. How does feature-gating work? Compile-time cfg attributes on the factory match arms.

title: Core Provider trait: message API, sync vs streaming, model parameters
description: Design the Provider trait for LLM API communication. Send messages, receive responses. Sync (full response) vs streaming (token-by-token) variants — or unified? Model parameters: temperature, max_tokens, top_p, stop sequences. System prompt handling. Tool schema passing. Response parsing (text, tool_use blocks, thinking blocks). How does the trait handle provider-specific features (Anthropic thinking, OpenAI function calling)?

title: Tool ↔ Permission integration: when and how PermissionGate checks tool calls
description: How does the permission system interact with tool dispatch? Per-tool permission classification (read/write/execute/network). Does the Gyre check before dispatch, during, or both? How does ActionKind map to ToolDef? Should ToolDef carry a permission classification field? How does dynamic tool loading affect permission policies (new tool = unknown permission level)?

title: Permission prompt UX: approve/deny/pattern/session/always graduated flow
description: Design the HITL permission prompt interaction. Study claude-code's '[y] once [p] pattern [s] session [a] always [n] deny' flow. How does this interact with ApprovalCache? Inline prompt vs modal dialog. How to show context (what the tool will do) without overwhelming. Timeout behavior (auto-deny after N seconds?).

title: Streaming output rendering: token display, markdown, syntax highlighting, spinners
description: How does streaming LLM output render in the terminal? Investigate: token-by-token display vs line-buffered. Markdown rendering (pulldown-cmark + crossterm). Syntax highlighting (syntect or tree-sitter). Spinner/progress for tool execution. Split pane for multi-agent? Depends on gyres-ms1 streaming decision.

title: REPL architecture: input handling, command parsing, readline, history
description: REPL design for the gyres CLI. Study claude-code and clawspring REPL patterns. Investigate: rustyline vs reedline vs crossterm raw mode. Command history persistence. Multi-line input (bracketed paste). Input preprocessing (slash commands vs agent input). Interrupt handling (Ctrl+C during agent step vs during input).

title: Design orchestration Gyre API: minimal surface from patterns vut-a through vut-d
description:
Synthesize findings from supervisor, swarm, HTD, and debate patterns into a concrete API.

Design decisions:
- What goes in gyres-core vs gyres-multi? Do we need new core traits, or just Gyre impls?
- Do orchestration Gyres need access to AgentRegistry and MessageBus, or can they work with just Agent refs?
- Is there a common OrchestratorGyre base, or are patterns too different to unify?
- How does the orchestration Gyre manage multiple GyreContexts (one per sub-agent)?
- What's the Outcome type for multi-agent runs? Aggregated results? Per-agent outcomes?

Output: Concrete trait/struct definitions for gyres-multi. ADR documenting the design choice.

title: Hierarchical Task Decomposition: recursive decomposition, planner+worker split
description:
Deep dive into Hierarchical Task Decomposition (HTD) for multi-agent systems.

Investigate:
- Architecture: planner agent decomposes high-level tasks, worker agents execute leaf tasks
- Recursive decomposition: when is a task atomic enough? Who decides?
- Depth limits: maximum decomposition depth to prevent infinite recursion
- Dynamic re-planning: what happens when a leaf task fails? Re-decompose the parent?
- Compare: HuggingGPT, TaskWeaver, ADaPT, tree-of-thought

Discussion questions:
- Is the planner a separate Agent, or a planning Strategy applied to any Agent?
- How does decomposition interact with TaskStore? Planner creates child tasks in the store?
- Should decomposition produce typed subtasks (code task, research task) or generic tasks?
- How does the HTD Gyre know the entire task graph is complete (not just one branch)?

Output: HTD Gyre design sketch with decomposition API and planner/worker interaction.

title: Swarm pattern: peer-to-peer, self-selecting work, task claiming, shared state
description:
Deep dive into the Swarm orchestration pattern for multi-agent systems.

Investigate:
- Architecture: no central coordinator, agents independently discover and claim work
- Task claiming: atomic claim operation on TaskStore (compare-and-swap on status)
- Conflict resolution: two agents claim the same task simultaneously
- Shared state: agents coordinate through shared MemoryStore (blackboard pattern)
- Emergent behavior: does coordination emerge from simple rules, or does it need explicit protocols?
- Compare: OpenAI Swarm (handoff-based), ant colony optimization, stigmergy

Discussion questions:
- How does an agent decide which task to claim? Random, priority-based, capability-matching?
- What shared state do agents need? Just TaskStore, or also MemoryStore?
- How do you prevent livelock (agents fighting over the same task)?
- Is the Swarm pattern a Gyre that spawns multiple inner Gyres, or something else?

Output: Swarm Gyre design sketch with claiming protocol and shared state model.

title: Supervisor pattern: coordinator delegates to workers, task assignment, failure handling
description:
Deep dive into the Supervisor orchestration pattern for multi-agent systems.

Investigate:
- Architecture: one coordinator agent delegates tasks to N worker agents
- Task assignment strategies: round-robin, capability-matching, load-balancing
- Result collection: how does the supervisor aggregate worker outputs?
- Failure handling: worker timeout, worker error, worker stuck. Retry, reassign, or escalate?
- Feedback loop: supervisor critiques worker output, sends revision feedback
- Compare: CrewAI (role-based delegation), AutoGen (group chat with admin), Claude Code's Agent tool

Discussion questions:
- Does the supervisor need its own Agent impl, or is it a Gyre that drives plain agents?
- How does the supervisor decide task-agent assignment? Capability matching vs explicit config?
- What happens when a worker agent produces a StepResult::Done but the supervisor disagrees?

Output: Supervisor Gyre design sketch with task assignment and failure handling strategy.

title: Design Task type: fields, status transitions, decomposition API, TaskStore integration
description:
Concrete design for the Task first-class type, informed by PM hierarchy (wy3a), Agile mapping (wy3b), and FDD/BDD (wy3c).

Design decisions:
- Task struct fields: id, title, description, type (epic/story/task/bug), status, priority, assignee (AgentId), estimate, acceptance_criteria, parent, metadata
- Status state machine: what transitions are valid? open→in_progress→blocked→complete→archived?
- Decomposition API: how does a Task decompose into subtasks? Does the parent auto-complete when children complete?
- TaskStore methods: create, get, update_status, add_dependency, ready_tasks, blockers, dependents
- Integration with beads: can gyres TaskStore backend to beads/dolt?

Output: Task type definition, status enum, and TaskStore trait methods.

title: Survey PM concept hierarchy: Theme→Initiative→Epic→Story→Task→Subtask type mapping
description:
Map the standard project management hierarchy to a type system for Gyres.

Investigate:
- How do Themes, Initiatives, Epics, Stories, Tasks, and Subtasks relate hierarchically?
- What's a node vs an edge vs metadata in a task graph?
- How do different PM tools (Jira, Linear, GitHub Projects, beads) model this?
- What fields does each level need? (priority, status, assignee, estimate, acceptance criteria)
- What status transitions are valid at each level?

Output: A proposed type hierarchy for the Gyres Task system, with clear node/edge semantics.

Refs:
- https://www.wrike.com/agile-guide/epics-stories-tasks/
- https://www.aha.io/roadmapping/guide/agile/themes-vs-epics-vs-stories-vs-tasks

title: Agent identity & capabilities: self-description for registry and task assignment
description: How agents describe what they can do. AgentCapabilities type, skill taxonomy, capability matching for task routing.

title: Project planning workflow: Agile mapping, FDD/BDD, Task type integration for native PM
description:
Map project management concepts to Gyres Task type for native PM integration.

Cover: Feature lists, PRDs, Requirements, ADRs, SPECs, PLAN.md, ROADMAPs, Projects, Themes, Stories, Sprints, Initiatives, Features, Epics, User Stories, Bugs, Issues, Tasks, RACI charts, GANTT charts.

Include Feature Driven Development & Behaviour Driven Development patterns, documents, artifacts, and components.

Goal: understand seams, hierarchy, and implicit side-effects we could pursue.

Refs:
- https://www.wrike.com/agile-guide/epics-stories-tasks/
- https://www.aha.io/roadmapping/guide/agile/themes-vs-epics-vs-stories-vs-tasks

title: Context assembly pipeline: memory→RAG→tools→artifacts→prompt with token budgets
description: How does data flow from sources to the agent's prompt? Pipeline stages: (1) Load persistent memory from MemoryStore, (2) RAG retrieval from ArtifactStore, (3) Tool schemas from ToolRegistry, (4) Session history from StateStore, (5) System prompt template from config/PromptStore, (6) User message. Token budget allocation: each source gets a budget, or priority-based filling? Who owns the pipeline — the Gyre, a ContextBuilder, or the Agent? How does it interact with ContextWindow (compaction when budget exceeded)?

title: AgentCapabilities: how agents describe what they can do for registry/task assignment
Research: RL environment aspects, features, and trait design
description: Research RL environment concepts to inform gyres-4mo (Environment trait). Topics: Gymnasium API (reset, step, render, close), observation/action spaces (Box, Discrete, MultiDiscrete, Dict), reward shaping, episode termination (truncation vs termination), vectorized environments (VecEnv, SubprocVecEnv), wrappers (frame stacking, reward clipping, normalization). Also explore: sim-to-real, environment composition, multi-agent environments (PettingZoo). What features must the Environment trait support for serious RL use?

title: Decision: commit to tokio or stay async runtime-agnostic?
description: Every async impl, store backend, CLI, and provider depends on this. Investigate: What are the actual constraints? WASM compat (tokio works in WASM with wasm-bindgen but limited), embedded use, library vs application. What we gain from tokio: spawn, sync primitives, fs, process, time, signal handling. What we lose: can't use in async-std, smol, embassy runtimes. What competitors do: rig uses tokio, most Rust async crates assume tokio. Recommendation likely: commit to tokio — it's the de facto standard and fighting it creates friction.
close_reason: ADR 0015: commit to tokio. Every Rust agent crate and key dependency already requires it. async-std deprecated. WASM constraints are platform-level, not runtime-level. Use specific feature flags instead of full. gyres-core stays runtime-free.

title: Multi-agent orchestration deep dive: full spectrum spawn→emergent coordination
description: Parent issue. Decomposed into: xo3 (supervisor), qvr (swarm), a3i (HTD), 362 (debate), 8w8 (API design). Full spectrum from simple delegation to emergent coordination. See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: CLI/TUI design: REPL, slash commands, permissions, streaming output
description: Parent issue. Decomposed into: ua6 (REPL), liq (streaming render), 92s (permission UX), kmv (slash commands). See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: CLI/TUI design: REPL, slash commands, permission prompts, streaming
Streaming: where token-by-token LLM output fits in Agent/Gyre/Provider
description: Streaming affects Agent trait, Provider trait, Gyre loop, CLI/TUI, and output routing. Key question: does Agent::step return a complete Action or a Stream<ActionChunk>? Options: (1) Provider streams internally, Gyre receives complete response — simplest. (2) step() returns impl Stream — most flexible but complex trait bounds. (3) Separate StreamingAgent extension trait. Also: how does streaming interact with tool-use (tool calls arrive mid-stream in Claude's API)? How does the Gyre render partial output while buffering?

title: Tool system: permissions integration, dynamic loading, MCP bridge
description: Parent issue. Decomposed into: m4c (permission integration), 27o (dynamic loading), 7ml (built-in tools). See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: Provider trait: streaming, rate limiting, failover, multi-provider
description: Parent issue. Decomposed into: u6x (core trait), egn (rate limiting), rsj (failover), 5fa (Anthropic impl). See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: Environment trait for RL agents (first-class RL path)
description: Design the Environment trait for RL agents. Classic interface: reset() → state, step(action) → (state, reward, done, info). How does this map to our Agent trait? Is Environment a peer of Agent (the Gyre mediates), or does the Gyre itself implement the environment loop? Vectorized environments (VecEnv): multiple parallel envs for batched step_batch. Continuous vs discrete action/observation spaces. Gymnasium/Farama API compatibility. Rendering/visualization hooks.

title: AgentRegistry + MessageBus traits for multi-agent orchestration
description: Design AgentRegistry and MessageBus traits. Registry: how do agents register? What metadata (id, capabilities, status)? Discovery: find agents by capability query. Lifecycle: who starts/stops agents — executor, supervisor gyre, self-registration? MessageBus: point-to-point, pub/sub, or both? Message types: task assignment, feedback, status update, custom. Delivery guarantees: at-least-once, at-most-once, exactly-once? Backpressure: what if an agent's inbox is full? Should these be on GyreContext (Optional) or passed separately to multi-agent Gyres?

title: Store abstraction: Backend trait, factory, feature-gated backends
description: Design the store abstraction layer. Already documented in docs/src/concepts/store-abstraction.md. Key decisions: Backend trait interface, StorageConfig → Stores factory, feature-gating strategy. Decomposed into subtasks: 7vv (Backend trait), ilc (factory), q75 (InMemoryBackend). This parent issue tracks the overall store abstraction design coherence.
close_reason: Store abstraction finalized: Backend trait + StorageConfig in core. MemoryStore, TaskStore, ArtifactStore traits added to gyres-core. Store-abstraction doc updated for single gyres-store crate with feature-gated backends. All four store traits + Backend compile clean. Child issues 7vv closed, ilc/q75 unblocked for gyres-store crate creation.

title: MemoryStore + GraphMemoryStore traits and GyreContext integration
description: Design MemoryStore and GraphMemoryStore traits for GyreContext. Already documented in docs/src/concepts/memory-architecture.md. Remaining questions: Is MemoryEntry schema correct — what fields are missing? How does embedding generation work — who calls the embedding model? Query API: semantic search vs keyword vs graph traversal — unified query or separate methods? How does MemoryStore interact with the context assembly pipeline (gyres-5y9)? Working memory vs persistent memory lifecycle.

title: M1: Finalize gyres-core types and traits
description: Implementation epic. Full plan in .claude/plans/2026-04-20-gyres-mvp.md with 11 tasks. Blocked by store abstraction (gyres-9mr), MemoryStore (gyres-deh), Task type (gyres-k1g), ArtifactStore (gyres-vz2) decisions.

title: Design RAG retrieval mechanics for ArtifactStore
description: ArtifactStore architecture is settled (RefStore, cross-type query view over shared storage). This issue covers the implementation mechanics: embedding strategy, ranking algorithms, chunking approach, hybrid search (FTS + vector), and how retrieval varies by Backend capability (SQLite FTS5 vs SurrealDB native vector). Refs: gyres-b1h (artifact taxonomy), docs/src/concepts/store-abstraction.md

title: Plugin API surface: what can a plugin provide?
description: What extension points do plugins have? Options: tools (ToolDef), strategies (Strategy impls), providers (Provider impls), store backends (Backend impls), artifact sinks (ArtifactStore impls), slash commands, telemetry sinks, permission policies (.polar files). How does the plugin register its extensions with the harness? Is there a PluginManifest type?

title: Plugin discovery and distribution: crates.io, git repos, custom registry
description: How are plugins found, installed, and updated? Options: crates.io (Rust-only, versioned), git repos (any language), custom registry (curated, searchable), npm-style (package.json equivalent). How does plugin metadata work (name, version, capabilities, required gyres version)? How does the user manage installed plugins?

title: Plugin architecture: trait-based, WASM, shared library, subprocess — compare
description: How do plugins load and execute? Options: (1) Rust trait objects via dynamic linking (.so/.dylib), (2) WASM modules (sandboxed, portable), (3) subprocess (any language, communicate via stdio/JSON-RPC), (4) compiled-in via feature flags. Compare: security isolation, performance, language support, developer experience. Study claude-code's skill system (subprocess-based) and MCP (subprocess). What's right for gyres?

title: Vector search integration: native DB support vs bolt-on (lance, qdrant)
description: MemoryStore needs semantic search. Options: DB with native vector support (SurrealDB has vector functions, Cozo has HNSW). Bolt-on vector index: lance (embedded columnar), qdrant (client-server). SQLite + sqlite-vss. Trade-offs: native = simpler, bolt-on = more flexible. What embedding dimensions do LLM embeddings use? Storage size implications.

title: Graph-as-library: petgraph, indradb — when is a library sufficient vs DB?
description: When do you need a database vs an in-memory graph library? petgraph: fast, in-memory, no persistence. indradb: persistent graph, simpler than full DB. GraphQLite: SQLite-backed graph. When is each appropriate? For gyres: agent knowledge graphs grow over time (need persistence), but may be small enough for in-memory during a session.

title: Embedded graph DBs: surrealdb, cozo, oxigraph — compare query, perf, Rust API
description: Compare embedded graph databases for gyres. SurrealDB: multi-model (doc+graph+kv), SurrealQL, embedded via surrealdb crate. Cozo: Datalog-based, embedded, pure Rust. Oxigraph: RDF/SPARQL, embedded, Rust. For each: query language ergonomics, graph traversal performance, memory footprint, Rust API quality, community activity, maturity.

title: Message passing patterns: point-to-point, pub/sub, request/reply, streaming
description: Which messaging patterns does the MessageBus need? Point-to-point (supervisor → worker), pub/sub (broadcast status updates), request/reply (ask agent for review, await response), streaming (continuous data flow). How does each map to inter-agent use cases? What's the minimal set for MVP?

title: Protocol comparison: MCP, A2A, custom — wire format and transport
description: Compare inter-agent protocols. MCP: JSON-RPC over stdio/SSE, tool-centric. A2A (Google): agent-to-agent, task-centric. Custom: what would a gyres-native protocol look like? Wire format: JSON-RPC, protobuf, msgpack. Transport: stdio, HTTP, WebSocket, Unix socket. What does gyres need for local multi-agent vs distributed?

title: Shared state patterns: blackboard, tuple spaces, CRDTs for multi-agent coordination
description: How do agents coordinate through shared state? Blackboard: agents read/write to shared MemoryStore. Tuple spaces: Linda-style coordination. CRDTs: conflict-free replicated data for eventual consistency. Which pattern fits gyres? Is MemoryStore sufficient as the shared state primitive, or do we need something more structured?

title: MemoryKind mapping: refine variants based on taxonomy and existing architectures
description: Informed by memory architecture survey and taxonomy research, refine the MemoryKind enum. Current variants: Fact, Preference, Decision, Procedure, Relationship, Working, Custom. Are these the right categories? Are we missing any? How does MemoryKind affect retrieval scoring and context injection priority?

title: Memory taxonomy: short-term vs long-term vs working, episodic vs semantic vs procedural
description: Map cognitive science memory categories to agent memory. Short-term (current conversation), working (active task context), long-term (cross-session). Episodic (what happened), semantic (facts/knowledge), procedural (how to do things). How do these map to MemoryKind variants? Should MemoryStore have different retrieval strategies per category?

title: Survey memory architectures: MemGPT/Letta, Zep, mem0, LangChain memory types
description: For each memory system: architecture, storage model, retrieval strategy, context injection method, strengths, weaknesses. How does each handle: memory limits, relevance scoring, temporal decay, cross-session persistence? What patterns should MemoryStore adopt?

title: Survey Python multi-agent frameworks: AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph, Swarm, CAMEL, MetaGPT
description: Deep survey of Python multi-agent frameworks. For each: architecture overview, orchestration pattern, agent communication model, tool integration, memory model, strengths, weaknesses, what gyres can learn from it. Focus on architectural patterns rather than feature lists. Which patterns are novel vs commodity?

title: Survey Rust/non-Python agent frameworks: rig, swarm-rs, Rivet agent-os
description: What exists in the Rust agent ecosystem? For each: what does it provide, what's missing, API quality, community activity, dependency weight. Also check Go, Java, C++ agent frameworks. Where is the Rust ecosystem weakest vs Python? What would make someone choose gyres over rig?

title: Research secret backend options: keyring, vault, 1password, encrypted file
description: Compare embedded vs external secret backends. Embedded: OS keyring (keyring crate), encrypted file (age, gpg, ssh). External: HashiCorp Vault, 1password CLI, passbolt. Evaluation criteria: complexity, security guarantees, offline support, CI/CD compatibility. Which should be the default? Keyring is simplest but not available in CI.

title: SecretStore trait: API design, scoping, rotation, expiry
description: Design the SecretStore trait. API: get_secret(key) → Option<Secret>, set_secret(key, value), delete_secret(key), list_keys(). Scoping: per-agent, per-project, global. Rotation: automatic key rotation support? Expiry: TTL on secrets? How does SecretStore interact with Config loading? Should Provider use SecretStore for API keys instead of config?

title: Anthropic provider impl: Claude-specific features, tool_use, thinking, caching
description: Implement the Anthropic/Claude provider. Claude-specific features: extended thinking blocks, tool_use content blocks, system prompt caching, prompt caching (cache_control), batched API. Anthropic SDK: use the anthropic Rust crate or raw reqwest? Streaming: SSE parsing for Claude's streaming format. Token counting: how to estimate before sending (tiktoken equivalent for Claude?).

title: Rate limiting and retry for providers: backoff, circuit breaker, queue
description: Where does rate limiting and retry logic live? Options: inside Provider impl, as a wrapper/decorator, or in the Gyre. Exponential backoff with jitter. Circuit breaker pattern (stop trying after N failures). Request queuing for rate-limited APIs. Per-model vs per-provider limits. How does this interact with error recovery (gyres-edw)?

title: Multi-provider failover: try A, fall back to B, health checks
description: Design for provider failover and redundancy. Provider selection strategy: primary/fallback, round-robin, lowest-latency. Health checks: how do you know a provider is down? Timeout-based, error-rate-based? Model equivalence mapping: if Claude is down, which OpenAI model is equivalent? State: does the failover provider need the same conversation history? How does this interact with model routing (gyres-woy)?

title: Dynamic tool loading: plugins as tool sources, MCP server tools, hot-reload
description: How do tools get added at runtime? Sources: plugin crates, MCP servers, user-defined scripts. Schema validation for dynamically loaded tools. Hot-reload: can tools be added/removed while the agent is running? How does this affect ToolRegistry thread safety? Tool namespacing (avoid collisions between MCP servers providing same-named tools).

title: Built-in tool set: which tools ship with gyres-llm, essential vs optional
description: Study claude-code's 40+ tools. Categorize: essential (bash, read, write, edit, glob, grep), important (WebSearch, WebFetch, Agent/subagent), domain-specific (NotebookEdit, LSP). Design the ToolDef interface for each essential tool. How much of the tool impl is in gyres-llm vs delegated to the OS? Tool output truncation strategy. Tool timeout handling.

title: Slash command system: registry, parsing, built-in commands, user-defined
description: Design the slash command system for the CLI. Study claude-code's ~85 commands — categorize by: essential (always needed), domain-specific (LLM only), user-defined. Command registry pattern (static vs dynamic). Argument parsing. Tab completion. /help generation. How do plugins register slash commands?

title: Debate/consensus patterns: multi-agent deliberation, voting, critique loops
description:
Deep dive into deliberation and consensus patterns for multi-agent systems.

Investigate:
- Debate: two agents argue opposing positions, judge selects winner
- Critique/review: agent A produces, agent B critiques, agent A revises (Reflexion-like)
- Voting: N agents propose solutions, majority or weighted vote selects
- Constitutional: agent self-critiques against a constitution/principles document
- Compare: CAMEL (role-playing), Society of Mind, debate decoding, Mixture of Agents

Discussion questions:
- How does debate map to feedback()? Is the critique a Feedback or an Observation?
- Is voting a Strategy parameter (how many rounds, vote threshold)?
- How does this interact with the Reflexion pattern we discussed for feedback semantics?
- Can debate/consensus be composed with supervisor or swarm patterns?

Output: Design sketch for deliberation Gyre variants with feedback routing.

title: FDD/BDD document types: feature specs, BDD scenarios, implicit side-effects
description:
Explore how Feature Driven Development and Behaviour Driven Development artifacts map to Gyres.

Investigate:
- FDD: Feature lists, feature sets, design-by-feature. How do these map to Task decomposition?
- BDD: Given/When/Then scenarios, .feature files. Are these Artifacts, Tasks, or test fixtures?
- Which of these should be implicit side-effects (agent produces them automatically)?
- How do feature specs relate to SPECs and REQs in the Artifact type system?

Output: Mapping of FDD/BDD artifacts to Gyres primitives, with implicit side-effect recommendations.

title: Map Agile ceremonies/artifacts to Gyres: Sprints, Backlogs, PRDs, ROADMAPs, RACI
description:
Determine which Agile artifacts become TaskStore features, ArtifactStore outputs, or Strategy config.

Investigate:
- Sprints: are these a time-boxed Strategy parameter, or a TaskStore concept?
- Backlogs: is a backlog just a TaskStore query (status=open, sorted by priority)?
- PRDs/ROADMAPs: are these Artifact types or composed views over the task graph?
- RACI charts: does this map to agent capabilities + task assignment?
- GANTT charts: is this a computed view from task dependencies + estimates?

Output: Classification table mapping each Agile concept to a Gyres primitive.

title: Secret management: keyring, vault, encrypted file, 1password, passbolt, PKI, mTLS, HSM/KMS, FIDO2, post-quantum
description:
API keys, MCP credentials, DB passwords can't live in plaintext Config/env.

Explore:
- keyring / secure-enclave
- vault (HashiCorp)
- encrypted file (SSH/GPG)
- 1password CLI
- passbolt
- post-quantum crypto
- PKI (SSL/TLS)
- mTLS
- HSMs or KMS
- Passwordless identity: FIDO2/WebAuthn

Need a SecretStore trait abstraction.

title: ZeroTrust inter-agent comms: microsegmentation, SPIFFE/SPIRE, fine-grained authz
description:
No implicit trust between agents. Every agent-to-agent communication must be:
- Microsegmented (agents can only talk to authorized peers)
- Passwordless (identity-based, not credential-based)
- Fine-grained, identity-aware authorization
- Auditable (full visibility into inter-agent interactions)

Explore SPIFFE/SPIRE for workload identity in multi-agent systems.

title: Cost tracking: per-agent per-task per-session dollar costs, token counting + pricing
description: Track costs per-agent, per-task, per-session. Needs: token counting (input/output per API call), pricing model (per-model rates from each provider), aggregation (roll up per-call costs to per-task, per-session). Where does cost data live — TelemetrySink attributes, a dedicated CostTracker, or MemoryStore? How does cost tracking feed into resource budgeting (gyres-4zi)? Should the Gyre abort when a budget is exceeded?

title: Backpressure: streaming, message bus, queue management beyond drop-on-overflow
description: What happens when output is faster than consumption? Streaming tokens faster than terminal can render. MessageBus filling up (agent produces faster than consumer reads). Telemetry buffer overflow. Options beyond drop-on-overflow: bounded channels with backpressure (block sender), priority-based dropping, adaptive rate control. How does this interact with streaming (gyres-ms1) and messaging (gyres-ycc)?

title: Rate limiting: provider-level, tool-level, agent-level throttling
description: Rate limiting at three levels: (1) Provider: respect API rate limits (RPM, TPM). Queue excess requests. Headers: x-ratelimit-remaining. (2) Tool: don't hammer filesystem or APIs. Configurable per-tool limits. (3) Agent: in multi-agent, prevent one agent from monopolizing resources. Fair scheduling. How does rate limiting interact with backpressure (gyres-yo6) and retry (gyres-edw)?

title: Documentation site: mdBook setup, examples crate, cookbook
description: Documentation site using mdBook. Structure: getting started guide, concept docs (already in docs/src/), API reference (generated from rustdoc), ADR index, cookbook with examples. Examples crate: gyres-examples with runnable demo agents. Cookbook: common patterns (basic conversation, tool use, multi-agent, custom provider, custom store backend). Deploy: GitHub Pages or Netlify.

title: Output routing: fan-out from Action to CLI/telemetry/artifacts/memory/next-agent
description: When an Agent produces an Action, it needs to go to multiple consumers: CLI (display to user), TelemetrySink (record span), ArtifactStore (emit decisions/docs), MemoryStore (store knowledge), StateStore (append turn), next Agent (in multi-agent). Who routes? Options: (1) Gyre does explicit fan-out, (2) event bus pattern (publish Action, subscribers consume), (3) middleware chain. How does this interact with streaming (partial Actions)?

title: Secret management: API keys, credentials, keyring/vault/encrypted store
Model routing: task-to-model assignment by complexity/cost/capability
description: Route different tasks to different LLM models based on complexity, cost, and capability. Example: Haiku for summarization, Sonnet for code review, Opus for architecture. Where does routing live — Provider, Strategy, or a dedicated Router? Routing signals: task type, estimated complexity, token budget, user preference. How does this interact with Provider failover (gyres-rsj)? Can the Gyre change models mid-conversation?

title: Agent trust model: identity verification and delegation trust in multi-agent
close_reason: subsumed by gyres-a4h (zero-trust covers trust model)

title: Research: MCP spec evolution — streamable HTTP, auth, remote servers, future direction
description: MCP is evolving fast. Research current spec and direction. Topics: Streamable HTTP transport (replacing SSE), authentication (OAuth, API keys), remote servers (not just local subprocess), server discovery, capability negotiation. How does this affect gyres-mcp design? Should we implement current spec or design for where it's heading? MCP SDK ecosystem: what Rust MCP libraries exist?

title: Research: rig crate — strengths, gaps, where gyres differentiates
description: Deep analysis of the rig crate (Rust AI framework). Architecture: how does it model agents, tools, providers? What's its Gyre/Strategy equivalent? Strengths: what does rig do well? Gaps: what's missing (multi-agent, permissions, memory, task planning)? API quality: ergonomics, trait design, error handling. Community: activity, contributors, adoption. Where should gyres differentiate vs where should it be compatible?

title: Graceful shutdown: flush telemetry, save session, close MCP, release locks on Ctrl+C
description: When the user hits Ctrl+C or the process receives SIGTERM, what happens? Ordered shutdown: (1) Signal in-flight agent steps to stop, (2) Flush TelemetrySink, (3) Save session state to StateStore, (4) Close MCP connections, (5) Release worktree locks, (6) Exit. How does this interact with tokio's shutdown signal? Timeout: if shutdown takes too long, force exit after N seconds. Depends on gyres-sk3 (CLI owns the signal handler).

title: Signal handling: SIGTERM, SIGHUP, SIGINT for CLI and daemon modes
description: Signal handling for CLI and daemon modes. SIGINT (Ctrl+C): graceful shutdown via gyres-8c4. SIGTERM: same as SIGINT. SIGHUP: reload config? Reconnect MCP servers? SIGUSR1/2: custom (dump state? toggle debug logging?). In daemon mode (Tier 4), signals are the primary control interface. How does tokio::signal integrate? How does this interact with the REPL (Ctrl+C during input vs during agent execution)?

title: Decision: WASM compatibility — browser, edge workers, WASM command packages (see rivet-dev/agent-os)
description: Should gyres target WASM? Use cases: browser-based agent UI, edge workers (Cloudflare Workers), WASM command packages (see rivet-dev/agent-os). Constraints: no filesystem, no raw sockets, limited threading (SharedArrayBuffer), tokio works but reduced (no tokio::fs, no tokio::process). What gyres crates could work in WASM? gyres-core (pure traits) yes. gyres-runtime (fs/git dependent) no. gyres-llm (reqwest works in WASM) maybe. Decision: WASM as a target or explicitly out of scope?

title: Decision: serialization formats beyond JSON (msgpack, bincode, protobuf) for RL throughput
description: JSON is fine for LLM messages but slow for RL state vectors (high throughput, many episodes). When does serde_json become a bottleneck? Alternatives: bincode (fastest, not human-readable), msgpack (compact, cross-language), protobuf (schema-evolved, cross-language). Should SerializedTurn support pluggable serialization format? Or should RL backends use native binary while LLM backends use JSON? Feature-gated: serde-bincode, serde-msgpack.

title: Logging strategy: levels, structured fields, format, tracing↔TelemetrySink correlation
description: How does gyres use the tracing crate internally? Log levels: ERROR (unrecoverable), WARN (degraded), INFO (lifecycle events), DEBUG (per-step detail), TRACE (internal plumbing). Structured fields: agent_id, session_id, span_id, tool_name, model, token_count. How do tracing spans correlate with TelemetrySink spans — same IDs or independent? Output format: human-readable (default), JSON (for log aggregation). Should gyres ship a default tracing subscriber or let users configure?

title: Deep dive: Agent Harness vs Agent OS — differences, seams, design implications
description: Explore the conceptual difference between an Agent Harness (runs one loop) and an Agent OS (manages memory, scheduling, identity, persistence, communication of many agents). Where are the seams? What's kernel (core traits) vs userspace (implementations)? How does this affect crate structure? Does gyres need a 'process model' for agents (spawn, kill, signal, resource limits)? Compare with Rivet agent-os, traditional OS concepts (scheduler, IPC, filesystem, permissions). Output: clear architectural boundary between harness and OS concerns.
close_reason: Research findings captured in docs/src/research/agent-infrastructure-landscape.md. Seams analysis in docs/src/vision/agent-os-vs-harness.md (rewritten). ADR 0014 records the decision: harness and OS are concerns, not products. Seven seams identified. Open questions documented for resolution during implementation.

title: Research: memory systems for agents (bleeding edge)
description: Parent issue. Decomposed into: vpb (existing architectures survey), odo (memory taxonomy), 75z (MemoryKind refinement). See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: Research: survey the multi-agent systems ecosystem
description: Parent issue. Decomposed into: 9rh (Python frameworks), sdv (Rust frameworks), u11 (research papers). See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: Research: context compression techniques (bleeding edge)
description: Survey context compression techniques for fitting more into limited context windows. Topics: summarization-based compression, embedding-based retrieval (only inject relevant context), sliding window with decay, structured extraction (facts not full text), AutoCompressors, ICAE, LLMLingua, context distillation. Which techniques apply to agent harnesses? How does compression interact with the context assembly pipeline (gyres-5y9)?

title: PromptStore type for prompt registry integration
description: PromptStore for managing prompt templates. Features: versioned prompts, labeled (production/staging/dev), cached with TTL, variable substitution (Mustache/Handlebars). Inspired by Langfuse prompt management. Is this a Store trait, or a utility on top of ArtifactStore? How does it interact with system prompt construction (gyres-0z5)? Should prompts be stored in the same backend as other stores?

title: Research: communication architectures for agents (bleeding edge)
description: Parent issue. Decomposed into: 3pt (message passing), dbs (shared state), bpl (protocol comparison). See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: Discuss prompting orchestration techniques for multi-agent systems in Gyres
description: How do you orchestrate prompts across multiple agents? Prompt chaining, prompt routing, prompt composition. How does a supervisor construct prompts for workers based on the task? Dynamic system prompts that adapt based on the agent's role in the current orchestration. Meta-prompting: using one LLM to generate prompts for another. How does this interact with PromptStore (gyres-rnm) and system prompt construction (gyres-0z5)?

title: Discuss model orchestration techniques for multi-agent systems in Gyres
description: Model orchestration for multi-agent systems. Topics: which model for which agent role? Heterogeneous model teams (Opus for planning, Sonnet for execution, Haiku for evaluation). Cost optimization across a multi-agent run. Model selection as a Strategy parameter. Dynamic model switching based on task complexity. How does this interact with model routing (gyres-woy) and Provider failover (gyres-rsj)?

title: Define agent affordances and how they unlock new capabilities
description: What are agent affordances — capabilities that an agent has access to that shape what it can do? How do affordances (tools, memory, communication, environment access) unlock emergent capabilities? Example: giving an agent file-write + bash + git = it can do software engineering. How do we model affordances formally? Are they the union of tools + permissions + environment access? How does this feed into AgentCapabilities (gyres-pj7) for task matching?

title: Research: embedded Vector+Graph DB options for agentic knowledge graphs
description: Parent issue. Shares subtasks with gyres-dvy: 4vd (embedded graph DBs), 40l (graph libraries), ubf (vector search), w69 (benchmark). See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: Research: GraphDB options (surrealdb, cozo, oxigraph, petgraph, sqlite, graphqlite)
description: Parent issue. Shares subtasks with gyres-0zb: 4vd (embedded graph DBs), 40l (graph libraries), ubf (vector search), w69 (benchmark). See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: Versioning strategy: lockstep vs independent, MSRV, semver across 7+ crates
description: With 7+ crates (growing to 12+), how do we version? Lockstep (all crates same version, release together) vs independent (each crate versions separately). Lockstep is simpler but forces releases of unchanged crates. Independent is flexible but creates compatibility matrix hell. MSRV policy: match latest stable? Pin to edition year (1.85 for 2024)? Semver: when is a breaking change? Trait changes, GyreContext field additions, new associated types. Pre-1.0 strategy: 0.x versions with more freedom to break.
close_reason: ADR 0017: independent versioning per crate, shared MSRV via workspace.package. Pre-1.0 strategy: 0.0.x during prototyping, 0.x.y when API is usable. Removed version.workspace = true from all 7 crates. Manual releases for now.

title: Testing strategy: mock providers, mock backends, property tests, integration
description: Testing infrastructure for gyres. Mock providers: deterministic responses, controllable failures, latency simulation. Mock backends: InMemoryBackend is the primary mock — is it sufficient? Property-based testing: proptest/quickcheck for permission chain (no combination of rules should panic), store operations (save then load = identity). Integration tests: real Claude API calls (gated behind feature flag + env var). Test fixtures: standard agent configs, sample conversations, permission policies. CI strategy: unit tests always, integration tests on demand.

title: gyres-tracing <-> langfuse-rs integration architecture
description: How does gyres-tracing consume langfuse-rs? Options: (1) Direct dependency — gyres-tracing implements TelemetrySink using langfuse-rs client. (2) Adapter pattern — gyres-tracing defines a LangfuseSink that wraps langfuse-rs. (3) OTEL bridge — gyres-tracing exports to OTEL, langfuse-rs is a separate OTEL consumer. Mapping: TelemetrySink spans → Langfuse observations. SpanId → Langfuse trace/span IDs. Attributes → Langfuse metadata. How does the tracing crate Layer bridge work alongside direct TelemetrySink usage?

title: System prompt construction: assembly from memory/tools/config/permissions
description: Who assembles the system prompt from memory, tools, config, and permissions? Is it the Gyre, the Agent, a dedicated PromptBuilder, or part of the context assembly pipeline (gyres-5y9)? What sections does a system prompt have? (identity, capabilities, rules, context, tools, memory). How does it interact with PromptStore (gyres-rnm) for prompt versioning? How are user instructions (CLAUDE.md equivalent) injected?

title: Error recovery and retry strategy across Gyre/Provider/Executor
description: When an LLM API call fails, a tool execution crashes, or a permission check times out — what happens? Who retries: the Gyre, the Provider, or the executor? Retry strategy: exponential backoff, circuit breaker, dead letter queue. Error classification: transient (retry), permanent (fail), degraded (fallback). How does this interact with rate limiting (gyres-hnn) and provider failover (gyres-rsj)? Should GyreError carry retry hints?

title: Config loading with figment: layering ~/.agents/ > .agents/ > env > CLI
description: Use figment crate for layered config. Layer order: defaults → ~/.agents/config.toml → .agents/config.toml → env vars (GYRES_*) → CLI flags. Config structure: provider settings, permission defaults, store backend, telemetry config, CLI preferences. How does per-project config override global? How does config interact with SecretStore (gyres-1wk) for API keys? File format: TOML (primary), JSON/YAML (supported via figment providers).

title: Versioning strategy: lockstep vs independent, MSRV, semver
Testing infrastructure: mock providers, mock backends, integration tests
gyres-tracing ↔ langfuse-rs integration design
System prompt construction: who assembles context from memory/tools/config
Error recovery and retry strategy across Gyre/Provider/Executor
Config loading: layering (~/.agents/ > .agents/ > env > CLI), file format
Plugin/ecosystem system with marketplace parity
description: Parent issue. Decomposed into: abc (plugin architecture), lhm (discovery/distribution), 2pl (API surface). See subtasks for scoped discussions.

title: Evaluate crate namespace strategy: Agent OS vs Agent Harness
description: Given the OS vs harness analysis (gyres-4zz), decide crate namespaces. Current: gyres, gyres-core, gyres-runtime, gyres-polar, gyres-llm, gyres-tracing, gyres-mcp. Proposed additions: gyres-memory, gyres-task, gyres-multi, gyres-rl, gyres-artifacts, gyres-plugins. Questions: should we claim these on crates.io now? Is gyres-multi the right name or should it be gyres-orchestration? Should store backends be gyres-store-sqlite or gyres-sqlite? Naming conventions for the ecosystem.
close_reason: ADR 0016: 10-crate namespace. gyres-store (single crate, feature-gated backends), gyres-orchestra (multi-agent), gyres-rl (RL). Dropped gyres-plugin, gyres-memory, gyres-task, gyres-artifacts. Hyphens, flat naming, no squatting.

title: ArtifactSink trait and GyreContext integration
description: ArtifactStore trait (readable + writable, not just a sink). Emit artifacts as agents work, retrieve via search for RAG context. Depends on gyres-9mr (store abstraction). Artifact types to define: Decision, Documentation, Plan, Specification, Note, Relationship (or use a generic Artifact struct with a kind field?). Integration with implicit side-effects pattern. How does the Gyre decide when to emit an artifact? Pattern-based, agent-signaled, or observer-agent?

title: Task as first-class type with TaskStore and implicit task planning
description: Make Task a first-class type in gyres-core. TaskStore trait with graph-shaped dependencies (many-to-many blocking). Depends on gyres-wy3 (PM workflow mapping) for type hierarchy and gyres-9mr (store abstraction) for backend integration. This issue tracks the concrete implementation: Task struct, TaskId, TaskStatus enum, TaskStore trait methods (create, get, update_status, add_dependency, ready_tasks, blockers, dependents). How does TaskStore integrate with beads as a backend?

title: M4: LLM agent loop with tool registry
description: Implementation epic. LLM agent loop with tool registry, provider integration, conversation Gyre. Blocked by Provider (gyres-ehz), tool system (gyres-rlu), streaming (gyres-ms1). Full implementation plan in .claude/plans/2026-04-20-gyres-mvp.md.

title: M2: Permission chain with Oso Polar fork
description: Implementation epic. Fork Oso Polar, build permission chain, approval cache. See .claude/plans/2026-04-20-gyres-mvp.md Task 4.

title: M3: Worktree-aware sessions and telemetry
description: Implementation epic. JSON + SQLite session store, git worktree detection, stdout telemetry. See .claude/plans/2026-04-20-gyres-mvp.md Tasks 5-7.

title: Benchmark: CRUD + graph traversal + vector search on realistic agent workload
description: Design and run a benchmark comparing the top 2-3 backend candidates. Workload: create 10K memory entries, 50K relationships, 10K vector embeddings. Queries: keyword search, semantic search (top-10), 3-hop graph traversal, session save/load. Measure: latency (p50, p99), memory usage, disk usage, startup time.

title: Survey research: multi-agent coordination papers, scaling laws, emergent behavior
description: Review recent research on multi-agent systems. Topics: agent scaling laws (more agents = better?), emergent coordination, communication efficiency, reward shaping for multi-agent RL, LLM-based agent evaluations. Key papers to find: multi-agent benchmarks, coordination protocols, failure modes at scale.

title: PKI/mTLS for inter-agent auth: certificates, identity, relation to zero-trust
description: How does certificate-based identity work for inter-agent communication? mTLS between agents on different machines (Tier 4). SPIFFE/SPIRE workload identity (related to gyres-a4h). Post-quantum considerations: when do we need PQ-safe key exchange? HSM/KMS integration for key storage. Is this the same discussion as gyres-a4h or complementary?

title: Migration paths: onboarding from claude-code, langchain, crewai, autogen
description: What's the onboarding story for developers coming from other frameworks? Claude Code → gyres: permission model mapping, tool compatibility, config migration. LangChain → gyres: chain → Gyre mapping, memory types, retriever → MemoryStore. CrewAI → gyres: role-based agents → AgentCapabilities, crew → multi-agent Gyre. AutoGen → gyres: group chat → MessageBus. What migration tools/guides would we need?

title: Research: A2A protocol and cross-framework agent interop beyond MCP
description: Research Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. What does it provide over MCP? MCP is tool-centric (expose tools to agents). A2A is task-centric (delegate tasks between agents). Are they complementary? Should gyres support both? How does A2A relate to our AgentRegistry + MessageBus design? What other cross-framework interop standards exist?

title: Discuss how large-scale RL on language models could work in Gyres
description: How could gyres support large-scale RL on language models? Topics: RLHF pipelines, PPO/DPO training loops, reward model integration, experience replay buffers, distributed training across multiple GPUs/machines. What would the Gyre look like for an RL training loop (not inference)? Does this require a different Agent trait, or is the existing one sufficient? How does this interact with the Environment trait (gyres-4mo)?

title: Ideation: finetuning Claude for max performance — what agent tools/harness is needed
description: What agent tools or harness features would maximize Claude's performance? Topics: extended thinking optimization, prompt caching strategies, tool schema design for Claude's strengths, system prompt patterns that Claude responds best to. How would you fine-tune Claude specifically for agent tasks? What training data would you need? Is this a gyres concern (harness optimization) or an Anthropic concern (model training)?

title: Ideation: scaled model evaluation framework driven by model-based eval techniques
description: Design a model evaluation framework that uses models to evaluate models. Topics: LLM-as-judge, rubric-based evaluation, pairwise comparison, constitutional evaluation. How does this integrate with gyres? Is an evaluator just an Agent with a scoring Gyre? How do you evaluate the evaluator (meta-evaluation)? Calibration: how do you ensure model judges are consistent?

title: Ideation: what would a novel agent harness look like?
description: Blue sky ideation: if you were designing an agent harness from scratch with no constraints, what would it look like? Provocations: What if agents were first-class OS processes? What if memory was a filesystem? What if tools were syscalls? What if permission was capability-based (like Capsicum)? What if the Gyre was a scheduler? Look at unconventional designs: Plan 9, Erlang/OTP, capability-secure systems, biological systems (immune system, neural networks).

title: Ideation: novel eval methods for multi-agent group problem solving
description: Novel evaluation methods for multi-agent group problem solving. Beyond individual agent benchmarks: how do you measure group coordination quality, communication efficiency, task allocation effectiveness, emergent behavior? Metrics: time-to-completion, message count, redundant work ratio, coverage (did all subtasks get done?). Can you use game-theoretic measures (Nash equilibrium, Pareto optimality)?

title: Research: agent performance benchmarks for coding and knowledge work
description: What benchmarks exist for evaluating coding agents? SWE-bench, HumanEval, MBPP, CodeContests. For knowledge work: research assistant tasks, document analysis, multi-step reasoning. How would we benchmark gyres agents against these? Should gyres ship a benchmark suite? How do multi-agent systems benchmark differently than single agents (coordination overhead, quality improvement)?

title: Research: should we create langchain-rs? Gap analysis
description: Gap analysis: does the Rust ecosystem need a langchain-rs? LangChain provides: chains (sequential LLM calls), agents (tool-using LLMs), memory, retrievers, document loaders, output parsers. How much of this does gyres already cover? Where are the gaps? Is langchain-rs a separate project or just gyres-llm with more integrations? Would it be a gyres plugin or standalone?

title: Research: should we create langgraph-rs? Gap analysis
description: Gap analysis: does the Rust ecosystem need a langgraph-rs? LangGraph provides: stateful graph-based agent workflows, conditional branching, cycles (loops), checkpointing, human-in-the-loop breakpoints. How does this compare to gyres Gyre + Strategy + TaskStore? Is langgraph-rs a separate project, or is gyres-multi effectively langgraph-rs? What does LangGraph do that gyres can't?

title: Checkpoint/rollback API for speculative execution and MCTS
description: Checkpoint/rollback API for speculative execution and MCTS. Agent::checkpoint() → CheckpointId, Agent::restore(id). How does this interact with &self on step (can you checkpoint shared state)? Does the checkpoint include MemoryStore and TaskStore state, or just Agent internal state? Is this an extension trait (CheckpointableAgent) or on the base Agent trait? Performance: how expensive is checkpointing? Copy-on-write vs full clone.

title: Resource budgeting: relationship to TelemetrySink/gyres-tracing
description: Resource budgeting: set token/dollar limits per-agent, per-task, per-session. How does this interact with cost tracking (gyres-b56)? Is it a PermissionGate (deny tool calls when over budget)? Or a Strategy parameter (budget as part of ConversationStrategy)? Or a GyreContext field (BudgetManager)? Can budgets be hierarchical (session budget split across tasks, task budget split across agents)?

title: ContextWindow type in gyres-llm
description: ContextWindow type in gyres-llm. Manages the bounded working memory for LLM agents. Token budget tracking (input/output), compaction triggers (threshold-based), compaction strategies (summarize old turns, extract facts, sliding window). How does ContextWindow interact with the context assembly pipeline (gyres-5y9)? Is it a struct on ConversationGyre, on LlmAgent, or standalone? Token counting: tiktoken-rs or provider-specific?

title: Stub Strategy implementations and explore seams
description: Stub out 3-5 concrete Strategy implementations to test the Strategy associated type design. Candidates: (1) ConversationStrategy (max_turns, temperature), (2) ExplorationStrategy (epsilon, decay), (3) ReflexionStrategy (reflector config, critique frequency), (4) ReActStrategy (reasoning+acting loop config), (5) DebateStrategy (num_agents, rounds, voting). For each: define the struct fields, sketch how the Gyre uses them. Find the seams: where does Strategy end and Gyre logic begin?

title: Multi-agent system observability: span fields vs new abstraction
description: In multi-agent systems, per-agent telemetry isn't enough — you need system-level observability. Is this just additional span fields (agent_id, role, task_id, orchestration_pattern) on existing TelemetrySink spans? Or does it need a new abstraction (SystemMonitor)? Visualization: message flow between agents, task progress across the graph, bottleneck detection. Compare with distributed tracing (Jaeger, Zipkin) — same concepts?

title: Brainstorm ADR/SPEC/REQ/PLAN/DOC/Zettel as Artifact vs Document trait
description: For each content type (ADR, SPEC, REQ, PLAN, DOC, Zettel), decide: is it a variant of an Artifact enum, or an implementation of a Document trait? Artifact enum is closed (gyres defines all types). Document trait is open (anyone can add types). Some types are structured (ADR has Status, Context, Decision, Consequences). Some are freeform (DOC, Zettel). Does the structure difference suggest enum for structured + trait for freeform?

title: Memory consolidation: extract ClawSpring pattern + research alternatives
description: Extract the ClawSpring memory consolidation pattern from ~/code/proj/rewrite/claude-code/clones/awesome/clawspring/memory/consolidator.py. Document: what triggers consolidation, what the AI synthesis step does, how it handles conflicts between memories, how it discovers relationships. Then research alternatives: MemGPT's memory management, Letta's tiered memory, RETRO-style retrieval-augmented consolidation. Output: comparison table + recommendation for gyres.

title: M6: Integration tests and re-export crate
description: Implementation epic. End-to-end integration tests, re-export crate verification. See .claude/plans/2026-04-20-gyres-mvp.md Task 11. Depends on all other milestones.

title: M5: MCP protocol bridge
description: Implementation epic. MCP stdio and SSE transports, tool bridge into gyres-llm ToolRegistry. Depends on M4 (LLM loop) and MCP spec research (gyres-4za).
